planting.note: Plant divisions or container plants in spring in moist, humus-rich, slightly acidic soil in partial to full shade; unlike the common bleeding heart (L. spectabilis), this native eastern species blooms from spring through hard frost if kept consistently moist.

directions: Plant root divisions in spring in consistently moist, humus-rich, well-drained woodland soil in partial to full shade; this eastern North American native handles the summer heat far better than the Asian garden bleeding heart and does not go summer-dormant as long as it receives regular moisture. Wild bleeding heart produces delicate, finely cut, blue-green ferny foliage and pendant clusters of small rose-pink to magenta heart-shaped flowers that continue blooming from late spring through fall frost under ideal moist, shaded conditions — one of the longest-blooming native perennials for deep shade. Cut back faded stems to encourage continued flowering and apply a mulch of shredded leaves to keep the root zone cool and moist through summer. An excellent companion for hostas, ferns, and astilbe under deciduous trees.

planting.note: Plant rhizome divisions in spring in dry to moist, acidic to neutral soil in partial to full shade; hay-scented fern is remarkably drought-tolerant once established and spreads to form large, weed-suppressing colonies — ideal for difficult shady slopes and dry woodland understories.

directions: Plant rhizome divisions or container plants in spring; this native eastern North American fern is among the toughest and most drought-tolerant of all ferns once established, colonizing dry, rocky, acidic woodland slopes where other ferns struggle. The light green, lacy fronds reach 18–30 inches and emit a distinctive, pleasant hay-like fragrance when brushed or crushed — one of the few ferns with a notable scent. Hay-scented fern spreads vigorously by creeping rhizomes to form dense, weed-suppressing colonies that are outstanding for naturalizing large shaded areas, suppressing invasives on slopes, and providing wildlife habitat; deer generally avoid it. It is deciduous, dying back cleanly in fall; cut fronds to the ground in late winter before new growth emerges.

planting.note: Direct sow in fall for natural cold stratification and spring germination; or start indoors with 4–6 weeks of cold stratification; Baptisia is slow to establish but extremely long-lived — a well-placed plant improves for decades and is one of the finest native perennials available.

directions: Sow seed in fall directly in prepared ground or stratify cold for 4–6 weeks before indoor sowing; seedlings establish slowly — expect little the first year and a full plant by year 3 — but once established Baptisia is virtually indestructible, drought-tolerant, and long-lived for decades. Blue false indigo is a magnificent eastern North American native prairie and woodland-edge perennial, producing upright 3–4 foot clumps of blue-green foliage and stunning spikes of deep violet-blue pea flowers in late spring that are among the showiest of all native flowers; the inflated dark seed pods that follow are ornamental into winter and beloved by flower arrangers. Tolerates poor, dry, rocky soils and light shade but flowers most abundantly in full to partial sun; once established, do not move or divide as it resents disturbance.

planting.note: Plant corms immediately after division in late summer or early fall in moist, humus-rich woodland soil; do not let corms dry out before planting; yellow trout lily is a spring ephemeral that emerges and blooms in early spring then goes fully dormant by early summer.

directions: Plant corms 3–4 inches deep in moist, leafy, woodland soil under the canopy of deciduous trees in early fall; yellow trout lily is a true spring ephemeral native to eastern North American forests, emerging from snow-covered ground in early spring with pairs of beautifully mottled green-and-brown leaves that resemble the markings of a brook trout — hence the name — followed by solitary nodding yellow flowers with reflexed petals and prominent orange anthers in March and April. The entire plant goes dormant by June as the tree canopy fills in, leaving no trace above ground until the following spring; plant in large colonies with later-emerging companions such as hostas or ferns to fill the gap. Slugs and snails are the main pests; avoid planting near areas of heavy slug pressure.

planting.note: Plant rooted cuttings or container vines in spring in moist, well-drained, slightly acidic soil in partial shade to full sun with a sturdy support structure; cross vine is named for the cross-shaped pith visible in a cross-section of the stem and is one of the finest native vines for southern gardens.

directions: Plant in spring in moist, well-drained soil against a sturdy trellis, fence, or tree trunk in partial to full sun; cross vine attaches by tendril-tipped branching structures that grip masonry, wood, and chain link effectively without damaging most structures. This southeastern native semi-evergreen vine produces spectacular clusters of 2-inch, trumpet-shaped flowers in reddish-orange with yellow throats that attract hummingbirds in profusion during its main spring bloom, with scattered rebloom through summer and fall; the glossy, compound leaves remain attractive year-round in zones 7–9 and turn burgundy-red in winter. Cross vine tolerates partial shade remarkably well and is an excellent choice for growing up the trunk of a large tree or covering a shaded fence where other flowering vines would fail. Prune lightly after spring bloom to maintain size and shape.
